evidence_quotes.quote: the gaming sector faces an equally insidious abusemost notably, the permanent disabling or delisting of games that have already been sold.
Personalized targeting exploiting vulnerabilities (e.g., financial or mental health).
Influencer marketing practices that may deceive buyers.

paragraphs.text: I write as a longtime advocate for the StopKillingGames community, to commend the EUs ambition in the Digital Fairness Act to curb dark patterns, addictive design, exploitative targeting, opaque subscriptions, and undue influencer practices. These measures will strengthen consumer control over their online journey and prevent unfair platform interventions. However, the gaming sector faces an equally insidious abusemost notably, the permanent disabling or delisting of games that have already been sold. Without a targeted safeguard, todays fairplay rules risk overlooking a digital kill switch that can destroy the value of a legitimate purchase overnight. The Acts existing clauses address: 1. Dark patterns forcing consumers into rushed choices. 2. Addictive design that mimics gambling to extract more spending. 3. Personalized targeting exploiting vulnerabilities (e.g., financial or mental health). 4. Subscription friction, making cancellations needlessly onerous. 5. Influencer marketing practices that may deceive buyers. All apply equally to gamesbut video games also form complex ecosystems of player communities, modders, streamers, indie studios, and preservationists. When a platform or publisher remotely revokes access to a gamewhether by removing it from storefronts, canceling keys, disabling onlineonly features, or bricking entire librariesit wipes out years of creative effort, community investment, and consumer trust. Proposed Clause6Protection Against Unilateral GameDisablement: 6. Publishers and platformoperators of videogame distribution, online services, or DRMprotected content shall ensure: a. Continuity of Access Consumers who have validly purchased a game (including DLC or expansions) retain perpetual, functional access to its core experience. Providers must guarantee offline modes in full, andwhere technically and economically feasibleoffer fallback solutions (e.g., released server binaries, official privateserver tools or documentation) to preserve essential onlinedependent features once the original service is discontinued. b. Transparent Policy & Notification Any change to servicedependent features (e.g. server shutdowns, DRM updates) must be published at least 90 days in advance, in both machinereadable and humanreadable form. c. Appeals & Redress A dedicated gamingsector appeals processstaffed by experts in game development and preservationwith binding decisions rendered within 30 days. d. Right to Repair & Preservation Publishers must provide fallback options (e.g., DRMfree executables, localhosted servers, or official patches) to maintain playability once a service is discontinued. e. Annual Transparency Report An accessible public summary of all game removals, service discontinuations, and DRM failures, including volumes, reasons, and postappeal outcomes. This paragraph dovetails with Clauses15 by directly safeguarding the lifecycle of a legitimately purchased gameprotecting consumers from digital killswitches that violate their right to repair, retain, and enjoy their purchase, fully in line with the EU ban on unfair contract terms under Directive93/13/EEC.
